text:
# Page 1^page=1
The Semantic Hub - Unifying Fragmented Semantic Dictionaries for Industry 4.0 and AAS Markus Rentschler, ARENA2036 e.V. Stuttgart, Germany markus.rentschler@arena2036.de Aaron Zielstorff, Fraunhofer IESE Kaiserslautern, Germany aaron.zielstorff@iese.fraunhofer.de Rico Schady FoP Consult GmbH Berlin, Germany rico.schady@fop-consult.de Frank Schnicke, Fraunhofer IESE Kaiserslautern, Germany frank.schnicke@iese.fraunhofer.de Sebastian Heppner RWTH Aachen University Aachen, Germany s.heppner@iat.rwth-aachen.de Marko Ristin University of Applied Sciences (ZHAW) Z¨urich, Switzerland rist@zhaw.ch Abstract—Semantic interoperability remains a critical yet un- derdeveloped foundation of Industry 4.0 and Asset Administra- tion Shell (AAS) ecosystems. Established reference systems such as IEC-CDD, ECLASS, ETIM, and UNSPSC provide valuable semantic content, but frequently lack direct web-resolvable iden- tifiers, open machine-readable APIs, and low-barrier publication mechanisms. This work analyzes these infrastructure gaps in detail and presents the Semantic Hub – an open, community-driven plat- form built on Wikibase. Acting as a central meta-dictionary and resolver service, the Semantic Hub complements existing dictionaries with persistent URIs, collaborative editing, and a unified retrieval service. This platform significantly lowers the barrier for publishing and resolving semantic definitions, paving the way for more scalable and interoperable digital twin ecosystems. Key contributions include: (1) a resolver/formatter archi- tecture supporting IRI, IRDI, and namespaced identifiers; (2) deterministic IDTA-native semantic identifiers; (3) improved governance processes for IDTA specifications; and (4) a live Wikibase instance operating under a CC0 license. Index Terms—AAS, asset administration shell, ontology, cross- domain, interoperability, semantic model I. INTRODUCTION The often-cited statement by mathematician and business- man Clive Humby – “Data is the new oil. It’s valuable, but if unrefined it cannot really be used.” – has become emblematic of the digital transformation. In today’s data-driven economy, the ability to transform raw data into structured, machine- interpretable knowledge is a decisive competitive factor. Modern industrial systems are characterized by ever- increasing connectivity and cooperation between machines, plants, and enterprise IT. While humans can often interpret ambiguous information using context and experience, ma- chines require unambiguous, semantically rich descriptions to act autonomously and correctly. Consequently, semantically unambiguous information modeling has become a key enabler for Industry 4.0 applications [1]. Semantic standards, however, are typically developed within specific domains or organizations. A single globally accepted standard is unrealistic in the near term. When heterogeneous systems exchange data, semantic mapping and alignment are therefore usually required. Semantic interoperability goes beyond syntactic compati- bility by ensuring that exchanged data carries a shared under- standing of meaning, including context, units, relationships, and concepts. It is essential for use cases such as flexible production, predictive maintenance, digital twins, and cross- organizational supply chains. Despite significant standardization efforts (IEC 61360, ECLASS, ETIM, etc.), a major infrastructure gap persists: many established semantic resources lack machine-readable web resolution, open publication pathways, and consistent versioning. This paper analyzes these shortcomings in the context of the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) and proposes the Semantic Hub – an open, Wikibase-powered platform together with a unified retrieval service – as a practical complement to existing dictionaries. II. RELATED WORK Interoperability in industrial applications is a widely dis- cussed topic, with a large body of literature addressing seman- tic interoperability in particular [2], [3]. Established seman- tic reference systems commonly used in industrial contexts include IEC-CDD, ECLASS, ETIM, UNSPSC, and others. These structured classification and description systems cat- egorize objects (e.g., products, components, and services) and their properties according to uniform criteria, assigning standardized identifiers and attributes that clearly define the meaning of data. By providing a common “language” based on hierarchical structures and semantic principles, such systems enable companies, industries, and technical systems to identify, compare, and exchange information unambiguously. For an AAS infrastructure to be truly effective in Indus- try 4.0, these semantic references must be accessible to all par- ticipants via the internet in machine-readable formats, in line with linked open data principles [4]. Relevant position papers and resources further elaborate on this, including the BDVA

# Page 2^page=2
document “Data Sharing Spaces and Interoperability” [5] and the International Data Spaces Association Information Model [6], [7]. The remainder of this section reviews the most relevant semantic guidelines, dictionaries, and their accessibility for Industry 4.0 and AAS-based applications. A. RDF The Resource Description Framework (RDF) was originally designed to represent metadata about Web resources (e.g., title, author, modification date, copyright, or availability schedules). By generalizing the notion of a “Web resource,” RDF can rep- resent information about any digitally identifiable entity, even if that entity cannot be directly retrieved over the network. B. VDI/VDE 2190-3 In parallel with academic work on semantic interoperability, the VDI/VDE-GMA Technical Committee 3.31 “Interaction of Industrial Data and Automation Systems” is developing guideline VDI/VDE 2190-3 [8]. The guideline structures ter- minology, concepts, and the scope of semantic matching to support semantic interoperability in Industry 4.0 applications. It explicitly addresses the challenge of heterogeneous se- mantic descriptions across system and domain boundaries. Semantic interoperability is shown to depend on more than the mere existence of identifiers; it also requires alignment of underlying structures, formalization levels, accessibility, and context. The guideline reflects the practical reality that in- dustrial interoperability draws from diverse semantic sources, including dictionaries, engineering models, ontologies, and other formal or semi-formal representations. These observations align closely with the problem addressed in the present paper: the coexistence of heterogeneous se- mantic sources and the limited machine accessibility of many existing vocabularies. This motivates the development of an additional open, machine-accessible infrastructure for seman- tic definitions that can complement established resources and enhance their usability in distributed AAS environments. C. Unified Namespace (UNS) A widely adopted best-practice approach for organizing semantic references across an industrial organization is the Unified Namespace (UNS). It is a vendor-neutral architectural pattern (rather than a rigid standard) that typically combines ISA-95 modeling with MQTT/Sparkplug B and/or OPC UA. The UNS organizes data according to the physical and opera- tional reality of the enterprise instead of being constrained by underlying communication protocols. Rather than using opaque tag-based addresses (e.g., PLC123.Tag456), a UNS employs human- and machine- meaningful hierarchical paths that inherently encode semantic context. The structure generally follows the physical and functional organization of the plant (site → area → line → equipment → parameter), most often based on the ISA-95 equipment hierarchy. In IIoT environments, the UNS is frequently realized through a hierarchical MQTT topic structure, for example: factoryA/assembly/line5/weldingRobot1/temperature Such path-based topics map naturally to URI paths, enabling unique addressing and integration. Typical URI representations include: urn:uns:acme:chicago:stamping:line01:press03: hydraulic:pressure and https://uns.acme.com/id/plant/chicago/stamping/ line01/press03/hydraulic/pressure Many implementations also expose the namespace via RESTful APIs, allowing IT systems (dashboards, ERP, ana- lytics) to query data using standard HTTP methods. D. IEC 61360-1 IEC 61360-1 provides a standardized information model for the definition of characteristics (properties). It defines core classes for properties, values, and value lists, and supplies for each characteristic a machine-readable identifier, definition, unit of measurement, abbreviations, symbols (when available), formulae, and references to further standards. Relationships between data elements are also supported. Although definitions are primarily in natural language, the semi-formal structure enables machine processing, automated unit conversion, and formula evaluation. IEC 61360-1 forms the basis for IEC-CDD as well as for AAS Concept Descrip- tions and embedded data specifications [9]–[11]. Fig. 1. IEC 61360 Data Specification

# Page 3^page=3
E. IEC-CDD The IEC Common Data Dictionary (IEC-CDD) [12] is an internationally maintained dictionary focused on electrical and electronic products. It is structured according to the IEC 61360 data model and includes classes, properties, value lists, units, and relations. Definitions use International Registration Data Identifiers (IRDIs), for example: 0112/2///61987#ABA565#009 refering to the value term “designation of the producer or manufacturer of a product” The dictionary is closed but searchable online 1 and offers XML export. No public API for fine-grained machine-readable resolution of individual definitions is currently provided. F. ECLASS ECLASS [13] (also styled eCl@ss) is a comprehensive dictionary for the classification of products and services, primarily used in e-procurement, ERP systems, and product catalogs. It is structured according to the ECLASS data model and employs IRDIs, for example: 0173-1#02-AAO677#004 refering to the value term “Manufacturer name: legally valid designation of the natural or judicial person which is directly responsible for the design, production, packaging and labeling of a product in respect to its being brought into circulation”. While sharing structural similarities with IEC-CDD, ECLASS focuses more strongly on commercial and business contexts. The full dictionary is subject to licensing, but indi- vidual definitions can be browsed freely 2ECLASS-Web. For AAS use, ECLASS publishes the Asset.xml file per release, which is explicitly referenced in IDTA Submodel Templates (e.g., Digital Nameplate). The joint ECLASS–IDTA guideline “How to transport ECLASS in the Asset Administration Shell” [14] provides detailed mapping rules and best practices. An API exists but is oriented toward database management rather than semantic resolution. G. GS1 Digital Link The GS1 system supports semantic referencing by trans- forming product and asset identifiers into web-resolvable re- sources based on Linked Data principles. This bridges physical identifiers such as the Global Trade Item Number (GTIN) with digital information for applications including supply chain traceability, product passports, and consumer engagement. GS1 Digital Link URIs follow patterns such as: https://example.com/01/{gtin}/10/{lot}/21/{ser}?17={ exp} The GS1 Web Vocabulary (WebVoc) 3 extends schema.org with GS1-specific classes and properties and supports JSON-LD. Conformant resolvers deliver 1https://cdd.iec.ch/ 2https://eclass.eu/en/eclass-standard/search-content 3https://www.gs1.org/gs1-web-vocabulary context-aware responses using typed links, enabling both human-readable and machine-readable content delivery. H. ETIM Technical Information Model The ETIM Technical Information Model is an international standard for structured technical product data, particularly in the electrical, plumbing, HVAC, and building installation sectors. It defines product classes, technical features (prop- erties), permitted values, and multilingual synonyms in a manufacturer-neutral manner. ETIM has been integrated into the buildingSMART Data Dictionary (bSDD), which assigns persistent URIs to ETIM el- ements. For example, the URI https://identifier.buildingsmart. org/uri/etim/etim/10.1/class/EC000003 resolves to the class “Earth leakage switch.” bSDD provides user-friendly search interfaces, a compre- hensive API (GraphQL and HTTP), a management portal, and open-source documentation on GitHub, significantly improv- ing machine accessibility: • Search interfaces: Interactive browsing of terms, classes, properties, and dictionaries is available via the bSDD Search Tool 4 and the dedicated ETIM Viewer 5. • API: The primary machine-readable interface (supporting both GraphQL and HTTP) 6, fully documented via Swag- ger at https://app.swaggerhub.com/apis/buildingSMART/ Dictionaries/v1 . • Management portal: A web platform enabling dictio- nary owners and maintainers to upload and manage content at https://manage.bsdd.buildingsmart.org/. • GitHub repository: Open-source documentation, data model specifications, import examples, and developer resources at https://github.com/buildingSMART/bSDD. The combination of ETIM’s domain-specific technical depth and bSDD’s open semantic infrastructure represents a sig- nificant advancement toward standardized and interoperable product data exchange in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) sector, resulting in numerous software tools with implemented support. I. UNSPSC The United Nations Standard Products and Services Code (UNSPSC) [15] is an open hierarchical classification sys- tem for products and services used in procurement, catalog management, and supply-chain applications. The dictionary is organized around a top-down hierarchy, form more abstract families to more concrete concepts. The UNSPSC uses a 10- digit code; each level of the hierarchy uses two digits (first 8 digits), with last two digits representing the business function. For example, the family 43230000 represents ”Software”, the family 43232900 stands for ”Networking Software”, and 43232910 for ”Wireless Software”. The dictionary is avail- able online at https://www.ungm.org/public/unspsc and can 4https://search.bsdd.buildingsmart.org 5https://viewer.etim-international.com 6https://technical.buildingsmart.org/services/bsdd/using-the-bsdd-api/

# Page 4^page=4
be freely exported to comma-separated table or accessed via UNGM (United Nations Global Marketplace) which pro- vides some helper endpoints via https://www.ungm.org/API/ UNSPSCs also for UNSPSC data. J. VEC Ontology The Vehicle Electric Container (VEC) [16] is a standardized data model for exchanging electrical and electronic system data in the automotive industry. Its primary serialization is XML, a complementary RDF ontology is available for Linked Data applications7. While the ontology can be downloaded as a single file, it does not support fragment-level HTTP resolution of individual definitions. K. IDTA and Semantic Referencing in AAS The Asset Administration Shell metamodel [9] uses the semanticId attribute (implementing the HasSemantics in- terface) to attach global semantic references to Submodels, SubmodelElements, and other model elements. A typical ex- ample is: "semanticId": { "type": "ExternalReference", "keys": [{ "type": "GlobalReference", "value": "https://admin-shell.io/idta/ CapabilityDescription/1/0/Submodel" }] } IDTA semantic identifiers follow a structured IRI syntax under the admin-shell.io domain. The specifications governing identifier construction are defined in: • IDTA-01001: Metamodel [9] • IDTA-01002: Application Programming Interfaces The supplementalSemanticId attribute allows additional links to external ontologies or standards, enabling richer semantic contextualization and integration with reasoning en- gines. L. AAS Concept Descriptions Asset Administration Shell Concept Descriptions provide a mechanism for an internal semantic dictionary per AAS repos- itory based on the IEC 61360-1 model [10], [11]. While early specifications referenced IEC databases, there is increasing use of ECLASS IRDIs and a dedicated guideline details the mapping of ECLASS content into the IEC 61360-1 structure for AAS [14]. III. ANALYSIS OF SEMANTIC REFERENCING IN THE AAS The AAS enables the semantic description of digital twins through mechanisms such as semanticId, supplementalSeman- ticId, and Concept Descriptions based on the IEC 61360 data specification. These mechanisms allow Submodels and Submodel Elements to reference globally identifiable semantic definitions and therefore establish semantic interoperability 7https://ecad-wiki.prostep.org/specifications/vec/v220/vec-2.2.0-ontology. ttl across systems and organizational boundaries. However, se- mantic interoperability requires more than globally unique identifiers alone. Applications additionally require standard- ized and machine-readable mechanisms to resolve semantic references into accessible semantic definitions. While the original focus of the AAS was strongly oriented toward IEC standards, recent specifications increasingly ref- erence ECLASS-based semantics. To support this transition, dedicated guidelines and mapping templates have been devel- oped for integrating ECLASS definitions into the IEC 61360 structure [14]. At the same time, current AAS ecosystems still rely heavily on legacy IRDI-based identification schemes and partially fragmented semantic infrastructures. Although modern web-resolvable IRIs are promoted conceptually, many semantic references still cannot be resolved directly via stan- dardized web technologies. This limits the practical usability of semantic references in distributed and web-scale AAS environments. A. IDTA Semantic ID Github Repository The central registry for IDTA-managed semantic iden- tifiers is hosted at https://github.com/admin-shell-io/id. It serves as a lightweight mechanism for maintaining the admin-shell.io namespace and registering new terms when suitable external vocabularies are unavailable. Identi- fiers are documented in semiformal Markdown files using a folder-based structure. While human-readable, the repository currently lacks a machine-readable dictionary service or direct URL resolution, limiting automated validation and discover- ability. It also carries quality issues and multiple definitions of identical terms. B. IDTA Submodel Template Repository The IDTA Submodel Template Repository (https://smt-repo. admin-shell-io.com/) provides API access to the templates us- ing base64-encoded IRIs. Responses returns whole submodel templates, which implicitly contain semantic information, but a dedicated retrieval of semantic data is not provided. IDTA URIs do not resolve directly via standard web requests. In- stead, they require interaction with a dedicated API endpoint, such as: https://smt-repo.admin-shell-io.com/api/v3.0/ submodels/{base64-encoded identifier} This design restricts seamless integration with semantic web technologies and automated discovery mechanisms. Clearly defined resolution mechanisms for IRDIs are missing. Com- bined with the fact that the IRDI-based semantic databases (IEC, ECLASS) currently do not provide suitable machine- readability and are under copyright restrictions, this creates long-term practical challenges for semantic interoperability in distributed, web-based environments. C. Challenges with External Semantic References The web services and online platforms provided by ECLASS and IEC CDD reveal several persistent limitations,

# Page 5^page=5
particularly when used for machine-to-machine (M2M) scenar- ios and in the context of Asset Administration Shell (AAS) applications. 1) ECLASS: The ECLASS content search and web service exhibit the following issues: • The textual search interface8 offers limited functionality and a sub-optimal user experience. • URIs or IRDIs used as semanticId cannot be resolved directly via the public ECLASS web service. • The available APIs are primarily optimized for inter- active, human-driven database management tasks rather than automated resolution of semantic references in AAS or digital twin contexts. • The JSON API endpoint unexpectedly returns XML responses in some cases, revealing inconsistent interface design. • When an IRDI is provided without a version specification, the service returns HTTP 400 or 404 errors instead of defaulting to the latest available version. • The web service certificate mechanism is not user- friendly, since individual purchase and installation is required. • Unauthenticated or unlicensed access typically results in a generic 404 error, providing insufficient diagnostic information. 2) IEC CDD: The IEC Common Data Dictionary (CDD)9 presents the following challenges: • There is no dedicated public API for resolving IRDIs. • No official, standardized URI scheme is provided by IEC that can be reliably used to derive URI-basedsemanticIds.Workarounds that rely on scraping dedicated HTML pages (e.g., https://cdd.iec.ch/cdd/iec61987/iec61987.nsf/ PropertiesAllVersions/0112-2---61987%23ABA565) are impractical. The server’s CORS policy restricts access to browser-based requests only, preventing web-based applications from reliably parsing the HTML responses. • Although XML downloads are available on individual HTML pages, this functionality is designed exclusively for manual, user-initiated operations and does not support automated or programmatic data retrieval. D. Limitations of Current Semantic Resolution Mechanisms The analysis shows that current semantic reference systems only partially support the requirements of interoperable and web-scale AAS solutions. Although semantic identifiers are widely used, their associated definitions are often not directly accessible in a machine-readable and standardized manner. Instead, semantic resolution frequently depends on proprietary APIs, manual downloads, or platform-specific mechanisms. This limits interoperability and complicates the automated processing of semantic references across organizational and domain boundaries. 8https://eclass.eu/en/eclass-standard/search-content/search 9https://cdd.iec.ch/ Another challenge is the lack of consistent resolution and publication mechanisms. Existing systems differ significantly regarding accessibility, version handling, machine readability, and openness of their interfaces. At the same time, the publi- cation of new semantic definitions is often slow or restricted by organizational and licensing constraints. As a result, the practical usability of semantic references in distributed digital twin ecosystems remains limited despite the availability of established semantic standards. These observations reveal the need for an open semantic infrastructure that complements existing dictionaries and stan- dards with standardized resolution interfaces and lightweight publication mechanisms. Such an infrastructure should support direct web-based access to semantic definitions while remain- ing compatible with established semantic reference systems and AAS-based applications. IV. SOLUTION The following core building blocks are proposed for a comprehensive semantic infrastructure aligned with Asset Ad- ministration Shell (AAS) principles: • IDTA Semantic Repository: Authoritative repository containing all semantic definitions defined in IDTA spec- ifications, particularly Submodel Templates (SMTs) and their individual properties. The ruleset and governance for the definition and content of both IDTA-native and foreign based (especially Non-IRI) semanticIds must be improved. • Semantic Wikibase: A community-driven platform for collaborative definition management. • Retrieval Service: A publicly available web service for resolving semantic definitions across distributed and heterogeneous repositories. It shall provide: – Search Interface: User-friendly web search for browsing terms and dictionaries. – REST-API: OpenAPI-documented machine inter- face for querying, integration, and publishing. – Management UI: Dedicated user interface for dic- tionary owners to upload and maintain content. A. Non-IRI Semantic Identifiers IRI and IRDI-based identifiers are inherently self- describing. However, many established classification systems, such as ETIM or UNSPSC, use plain codes that lack intrinsic context. For example, an arbitrary 8-digit number cannot be reliably identified as a UNSPSC code without additional metadata, as it does not have a built-in checksum, prefix, or unique structural property. To ensure unambiguous identification and resolvability, these identifiers must carry explicit namespace or type infor- mation. The recommended approaches are the following: • Namespaced notation: "unspsc:44121706" or "etim:EC000001" • IRI-based form (preferred when available): https://identifier.buildingsmart.org/uri/etim/etim/10.1/ class/EC000001

# Page 6^page=6
Namespace prefixes and the associated source information to where they can be resolved should in an AAS be defined in SMC ProductClassifications of SMT TechnicalData [17]. Additionally, it is recommended to maintain a central dynamic list of known reference systems to provide guidance to AAS implementors and allow applications hardcoded resolving of these established systems. B. IDTA-Native Semantic Identifiers The IDTA’s current AAS standardization environment is distributed across multiple GitHub repositories. This can result in redundant maintenance efforts, inconsistencies between Submodel Templates and semantic definitions, and reduced machine-processability, particularly when semantic references are not defined consistently down to the property level. In ad- dition, externally managed semantic identifiers, such as IRDIs, are frequently used for core structural and property definitions within SMTs, introducing additional dependencies on external semantic reference systems and versioning schemes. To address these issues, we propose IDTA-native semantic identifiers that are derived deterministically from the hierar- chical structure of Submodel Templates. Every AAS element becomes implicitly addressable by combining the Submodel Template identifier with the element’s idShortPath, i.e.: https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/ DigitalNameplate/3/0/ManufacturerName These identifiers are implicitly defined by the formal SMT specification itself and therefore do not require separate maintenance in additional semantic repositories. The formal Submodel Template thus becomes the single source of truth for the semantic structure and identifier hierarchy of stan- dardized SMT-defined elements. Since semantic identifiers are deterministically derived from the SMT structure and version, semantic references remain stable and reproducible across implementations while still supporting explicit version- ing through the SMT lifecycle. The proposed approach focuses primarily on standardized and template-defined structures. Existing user-defined, dy- namic, or externally managed references (e.g., ECLASS IRDIs or API-based identifiers) may still use explicit semanticIds or semantic mappings where required. Additional semantic references through supplementalSemanticId can still be used for ontology mappings, cross-dictionary relationships, or inte- gration with external semantic ecosystems. Key Advantages: • Reduced maintenance effort and fewer inconsistencies. • Consistent semantic identifiers down to property level. • Deterministic and directly resolvable semantic references. • Improved automated validation and quality assurance. • Single source of truth based on the formal SMT defini- tion. • Lower implementation effort for tools and applications. • Dictionary-agnostic core referencing with full external compatibility. • Complete backward compatibility with the existing AAS ecosystem. C. Governance Semantic definitions for IDTA specifications are currently maintained in GitHub repositories as hierarchically structured Markdown files with semiformal content. This often results in inconsistencies between documentation, Submodel Templates, and semantic repositories. Proposed Improvements: • Manage definitions in standardized machine-processable formats (RDF, Turtle, or OWL). • Represent concepts as RDF-based Concept Descriptions enriched with ontology relationships (isCaseOf, etc.). • Implement automated consistency checks between SMTs and the semantic repository. • Introduce CI/CD pipelines with quality gates (duplicate detection, validation of references, semantic consistency, etc.). • Preserve provenance, ownership, and licensing metadata for externally referenced semantic definitions. • Achieve direct web-resolvability of IDTA semanticIds or via a native IDTA semantic service. This governance model significantly increases reliability, maintainability, and interoperability while supporting both for- mal standardization and community contributions. At the same time, provenance and licensing information can be preserved for externally referenced semantic content originating from standards organizations or proprietary dictionaries. D. Semantic Hub The Semantic Hub (https://semantic-hub.io/) is a collabora- tive, open and community-driven platform that acts as a central meta-dictionary for industrial semantic resources. It addresses the widespread fragmentation of semantic definitions across disparate dictionaries, standards, APIs, and ontologies by providing a unified, searchable, and extensible repository (see Fig. 2). Fig. 2. Architectural Concept of the Semantic Hub 1) Core Concept and Objectives: The Semantic Hub aggre- gates and harmonizes semantic resources from standardization bodies, industry consortia, open communities, and existing dictionaries. By leveraging structured data technologies, the platform enhances semantic interoperability, enables consis- tent data mapping, supports automated consistency checking, and facilitates unambiguous communication across industrial ecosystems.

# Page 7^page=7
2) Technical Foundation and Features: The platform is built on Wikibase 10 (the software powering Wikidata11) and offers the following key capabilities: • A structured, queryable knowledge base with full SPARQL and Linked Data support. • Persistent, globally unique semanticIds for every concept, available as stable URIs/IRIs. • A dual provenance model: – External definitions: imported from official standards and other sources while preserving original wording, context, and licensing through provenance qualifiers. – Internal/community definitions: collaboratively de- veloped and maintained, serving as candidates for future formal standardization. • Advanced full-text search, property-based querying, and specialized tools such as “GoBySemanticId”. • Integrated community governance via the associated discussion platform (https://semantic-hub-community. coapp.io/). All internally created or curated definitions are published under the CC0 (public domain) license to maximize reusability and promote true semantic interoperability. 3) Implementation and Governance: The Semantic Hub is operated by FoP Consult GmbH (Berlin) and follows an open contribution model combined with rigorous provenance tracking and quality control by a community. This hybrid approach positions the platform as a trusted bridge between formal standardization organizations and industrial practition- ers. 4) Significance: The Semantic Hub provides a practical, living semantic infrastructure that transforms fragmented do- main knowledge into a single, machine- and human-readable hub. By lowering integration barriers and accelerating ontol- ogy alignment, it enables the development of more interop- erable industrial systems and federated data ecosystems. The platform is particularly valuable for researchers and engineers working on semantic integration, digital twins, and cross- domain data exchange. E. Retrieval Service 1) Architecture: From an architectural viewpoint, the enti- ties resolver and formatter need to be implemented to identify and translate the different semantic sources: a) Resolver: The resolver identifies the appropriate se- mantic source for a given semanticId and provides access to its endpoint. It retrieves the semantic information from the external resource and maps it to the IEC 61360 datatemplate format. This concept generalizes previous work on the dis- covery of semantic resources for semantically heterogeneous Asset Administration Shells [18]. While the original approach focused on locating endpoints for semantic matching, the same discovery mechanism can be applied more broadly to arbitrary semantic sources and providers. The issuing organization can 10https://wikiba.se/ 11https://www.wikidata.org/ Fig. 3. Flowchart of the resolver for identifying a suitable endpoint for a given semanticId. publish a reference to the appropriate semantic endpoint using a DNS TXT record. In industrial practice, semantic IDs are typically expressed as either IRIs, IRDIs or other ID types. The resolution process, illustrated in Fig. 3, handles possible schemes as follows: • IRI-based semantic IDs: The resolver analyses the authority component of the IRI and performs a domain- specific lookup. • IRDI-based semantic IDs: Since IRDIs do not directly map to Internet domains, a dedicated registry maps reg- istration authorities (identified by codes such as 0112 for IEC-CDD or 0173 for ECLASS) to corresponding endpoints. • Other ID types: To make such semanticIds identifiable, context and metadata with an explicit type or names- pace is required, e.g. by using a typed field prefix like "unspsc:44121706" or "etim:EC000001". Once a suitable endpoint has been identified, the resolver retrieves the requested semantic information and forwards it to the formatter. If no source-specific endpoint is found, the resolver falls back to a configurable default endpoint. This design enables uniform access to heterogeneous semantic sources through a single, extensible discovery mechanism. b) Formatter: The formatter transforms heterogeneous source representations (XML dictionaries, RDF/OWL ontolo- gies, proprietary APIs, etc.) into a unified, consumable format. It implements lightweight, rule-based model transformations, using the IEC 61360 data template as the default target for AAS compatibility. Key capabilities include: • Support for multiple output formats (default 61360, full source structure, enriched HTML). • Preservation of provenance and source references. • Query-driven response shaping. This design enables uniform, reliable access to semantic infor- mation across diverse industrial ecosystems while generalizing earlier discovery approaches for semantically heterogeneous AAS. The transformation process follows predefined mapping rules that depend on the respective semantic source. These

# Page 8^page=8
rules define how source-specific constructs are interpreted and how they are represented in the target format. Since semantic descriptions are often incomplete or differ- ently structured, the transformation is not lossless in all cases. The formatter therefore prioritizes transparency by preserving source references and, where applicable, exposing the origin and structure of the underlying data. F. Implementation Status The Semantic Hub platform (https://semantic-hub.io/) is operational and based on Wikibase. The public in- stance, including the community discussion platform (https: //semantic-hub-community.coapp.io/), is already accessible. The central retrieval service (resolver + formatter) has been prototypically implemented and successfully tested with se- lected entries from VEC, ETIM/bSDD, and IEC-CDD. IDTA- native identifier generation logic has been formalized and is being integrated into IDTA tooling. Broader dictionary imports, full production rollout of the resolver service, and deeper integration with IDTA processes are ongoing within the funded projects “Transformationshub Leitungssatz” and “OPEN”. V. ACKNOWLEDGEMENT The authors appreciate the support from the German Fed- eral Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy (BMWE) through the project ”Transformationshub Leitungssatz” (Grant No. 16THB0003A, https://www.leitungssatz-hub.de/en/) and the German Federal Ministry of Research, Technology and Space) (BMFTR) through the project ”OPEN” (https:// openengineeringspace.org/), funded as part of the DATIpilot programme (Module 2 – Innovation Communities). REFERENCES [1] C. Diedrich, T. Hadlich, and M. Thron, Semantik durch Merkmale f¨ur Industrie 4.0. Berlin, Heidelberg: Springer Berlin Heidelberg, 2024, pp. 333–348. [Online].
